story:
Soap slides off a black plate in slow, lemon-scented beads, and rain hammers the road so hard it looks like it’s bouncing back into the sky. This is my life now: Blue Moon Diner, Roswell, New Mexico, fall of 1998. My badge says Henrietta, but it should say Dishwasher. Customers talk slow at me until I speak and they realize I understand English just fine.

It wasn’t meant to be my life. I had another one, with purpose. I was meant to go back—not to here, but to 2098, to a small city in England called Chester, where a resistance believed stopping Anna Stewart before birth would stop a catastrophe in my century. It wasn’t about glory; it was a desperate, ugly order to prevent a future of ruin.

Instead, I woke up in a hospital in the American Southwest, 1998, my head throbbing, my mission already ruined. My intel only covered the late twenty-first century; I knew almost nothing about this time, except the handful of twentieth-century disasters everyone in my era learned by date. In a panic I bolted, because who would believe a time traveler? I had no proof besides the sleek pistol the team issued me—clean shot, no trace, advanced enough to scare me. Just before a car clipped me, I’d thrown my backpack under a dumpster. When I found it later, I hid the gun under the Blue Moon’s old fridge after Simon, the manager with a sandpaper voice and soft heart, hired me on September 2. He didn’t believe my story, but he pitied me, and he wasn’t unkind.

I told him to watch the news on October 3. If the plane crash I remembered hit when it should, he’d know I wasn’t crazy. October 4 arrived. No crash. No validation. I wasn’t just in the wrong year; I was in a reality with the dials turned a notch to the side. Simon asked me—gently this time—to see a professional. Maybe I had been in the psych ward. Maybe the car had knocked something loose. I snapped and left him in the kitchen. The diner air smelled like coffee and sweat; for a second I loved it.

I crouched by the rusty fridge, dug through sticky dust and old food, and felt panic flood in: the gun was gone. Gary had cleaned the floor the other night. Maybe he’d found it. Simon shouted that a woman had just walked in, would I wash my hands and serve her while he fetched his cigarettes? I wiped at my tears and went out front.

She was British, soft-voiced, impossibly beautiful. And wrong somehow: her eyes glowed too bright. “Henrietta Welling?” she asked, though my badge didn’t show my last name. The silver pistol she drew was a twin of mine, intricate and cold. She said her name was Sara. She said the trip was one-way, same as mine. She said I’d messed up—not the machine’s drift into 1998, not this alternate history, but the way losing my weapon had started a chain of events that would let this world punch through and start a war with ours.

She held up my pistol. She’d already tested it—on Gary. If she was stuck here on a mission to contain the damage I’d caused, she’d make sure I couldn’t make it worse.

She raised the gun. White light bloomed. It felt like falling forever.

After, the fat chef asked the woman where I’d gone. “She swore nobody believes her,” she said, calm and sad, “and ran out.” He shrugged like that happened every week. The woman’s eyes tracked the room—cheap coffee, primitive tech, a place you could disappear while you waited.

“I hear there’s a vacancy at the Blue Moon Diner,” she said.

And just like that, she took my place.
